speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's February 23rd, 1997. This is the interview with Alexander Riseman in Hendon, London, England. And the interview is done by David Kat. Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could I have your, your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Alexander David Riseman. It's-- in England, it's spelled R-I-S-E-M-A-N. My original name is spelled different. In Poland, it was spelled R-A-J-Z-M-A-N

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the 15th of October, 1926.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lodz, which was the second largest city in Poland. It had a population of about 800,000 people. A third of them were ethnic Germans, and a third were Jews, and the rest were Poles. Lodz was a very highly industrialized city. It was called the Manchester of Poland-- of Europe. It had the second largest Jewish community in Poland-- in Europe. The first-- the largest was Warsaw. And Lodz was the second largest. The Jews were mainly employed in the textile business, together with the Germans. The Germans were mainly employed in--  in the weaving of the cloth. And the Jews were, were in, in the finishing and the manufacturing of the finished garments. Lodz was originally a very small town. It only became industrialized at the-- at the turn of the 19th century. The Russian government, under the czar, wanted to industrialize Poland. So they brought over German weavers into Lodz. And they set up factories, textile factories. And gradually, the Jews also settled in Lodz. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll, we'll get to the, to the, to the wider picture in a second. I would like to know a bit more about your family. Who was, who was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was called Pinchas Riseman.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born at the turn of the 20th century. I can't remember the exact date when he was born. And, I know when he was killed later on, he was about 40 or 41 years old. And that was 19-- 1940. So he must have been born at the turn of the century. My mother was about two years younger than him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Lea. Her maiden name was Engel. E-N-G-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have-- did you have brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- we were three children at home. My older-- I had an old sister. Her name was Rachel. She was two years older than me. And I was the middle child. And then I have a younger brother. His name is Yitzhak. And he lives-- he's alive. He survived with me. And he lives now in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about your first childhood memory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what I can remember from my childhood, it was a very happy childhood. We, we had a normal childhood. And as far as I knew, my parents got on very well amongst themselves. And we had a large-- lots of cousins, and uncles, and aunts. And my, my, my par-- my father's parents were still alive. They lived in Lodz. But my mother's parents, they came from a little town. It was called Szydlow in Poland. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Polish, I think it was spelled S-Z-Y-D-O-V. Szydlow. And my-- my grandfather, I was told, was the-- the shochet in that little town. He was-- a shochet is a-- is a-- a ritual slaughterer. He has to be a well learned man, otherwise he can't perform the-- the ritual killing of the animals or, or birds. And he was usually well-respected in the community. He was usually the second most respected person after the rabbi in the town. So-- and I'm named after-- his name was Alexander, and I'm named after him. Because as you know amongst Jews, we do not name-- amongst Ashkenazi Jews-- we do not name the children if the people are still alive. So because he was already dead when I was born, so they named me after him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe your-- the Jewish part of your-- of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were very orthodox. Although my father did not have a beard, which in Poland it was very unusual amongst Orthodox Jews. They usually had beards. My father was clean-shaven because he was a businessman. And-- But we were very orthodox, and we went to a very orthodox school. It was a very large school. It was one of the most expensive schools. The Jewish schools were all-- had to be paid for. It was not supported by the government. And I-- as far as I remember, it-- it-- it cost my father a lot of money to send us to this school. Lodz had a lot of Jewish schools. It was a population of a quarter of a million Jews. You can imagine. It also had a very cultural-- very highly developed cultural life-- Jewish cultural life, I'm talking about. It had a few daily Jewish-- Yiddish newspapers. It had lots of periodicals and weeklies and monthlies, some of them orthodox papers, and some of them non-orthodox, and Zionistic papers. And there lots of-- a few Jewish theaters and many libraries. There was a highly developed Jewish cultural life in Lodz. And-- [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I went to a school where the, the children started from the age of three in kindergarten. And we carried on in that school till the children were 18. They had many classes. It must have had maybe thousand or maybe more pupils, that school. It was not far from us, that school. We started school about half past 8:00 in the morning. And we carried on till half past 2:00. Then, we went home for our lunch. And we went back 4 o'clock. And we carried on-- we were in school until 8 o'clock. We had a break in the middle, but we carried on until 8 o'clock. And then we came home. And we still had to do some homework. We did not have time for-- to do a lot of sport and things like that. We were always busy with all kinds of lessons.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you study in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of classes did you have in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had secular subjects for about three or three and a half hours a day. Polish-- every day we had to have a Polish lesson, the Polish language. Math, geography, history, Polish history, Jewish history. The rest of the studies were concentrated on religious, the Talmud, mainly. Everybody-- every day we had a few lessons in Talmud. So when a boy reached the age of 13 or 14, he could learn already by himself the Talmud, which is not an easy task, as one can imagine.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no girls in your school.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was only a boys' school. Girls-- my sister went to a state school. In those days, girls were not-- it was not considered for girls to have a very high Jewish education. As long as they could read a Jewish paper, and they could pray, and they could write a Yiddish letter-- my father used to employ a man. He used to come into the house every day and for an hour or two to teach my sister the Jewish subjects because she went to a state-- Polish state school, so she didn't learn anything in the-- about Jewish culture or the Jewish language, or anything. So she had to have a teacher in--  in the house, private. Also, we went to school six days a week. And we had-- the Sabbath, we had free. But even the Sabbath, we were not completely free. We had to go-- one was sent from one parent to the other to be tested what we learned the whole week. And after the Sabbath was out, we had to collect from this person a note, and give it on Sunday to the teacher, that we knew what we were-- what we learned the whole week. So even on Sabbath, we-- we had to go for an hour or however long it took to be tested to this person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Education was, was very important in your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: --childhood years.

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we, we-- even people that could hardly afford to-- to eat sent their children to a Jewish school to have a Jewish education. Jews always-- as today, I think it's the same. Myself, I spent a lot of money on educating my children. If I wanted them to know-- to have a Jewish education, I sent them to private schools. And it was a very expensive part of my-- I spent a lot of money-- part of my earnings. But we always considered that to be important, so that's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: All your friends in school were-- all your friends-- all your friends were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school were only Jewish. Yes. It was a Jewish school mainly for Jews. Yes. In the summer, we used to go-- we had very long school holidays. They started from the end of May till the end of July, nearly three months. And my father used to rent a cottage in the country. And he used to-- he used to send us away with my mother. And he used to come out for the weekends only. And even on the holiday, he didn't leave us alone. He rented-- he hired a, a man to teach us a few hours a day. And-- but it was nice. It was a nice life. And we, as children, we enjoyed it. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In the country, on-- on Saturday night, we used to have, I remember-- we used to have a gramophone with a handle that you had to turn up with, with records. And we used to play a lot of Jewish records, Hazzanut records, all kinds of classical music. That was the entertainment. In-- at home, in town, we did have a radio. In those days, a radio was really considered a luxury. We also had a telephone, which was very unusual in those days in Poland. So in other words, we were quite-- we were not-- I wouldn't say we were very rich, but we were comfortable. My father made a very good living. We had a textile shop in, in, in, in the center of Lodz. And we lived in a good area where the rents were very expensive. We had two rooms and a kitchen, which was already considered a good-- a good size-- a good size apartment in that area. The rents were very expensive. We had my-- two bedrooms and a kitchen. And then we had another room next to it, sort of a living room. My parents slept in one bedroom. And me and my brother slept in the other bedroom. And my sister slept in the kitchen on the put-you-up bed, which was put together for the day and they brought out-- was bought out for the night for her to sleep. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about a, a typical Shabbat, for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us about how Shabbat was celebrated, for example, in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat was celebrated Friday night. Everybody had put on their, like you say in English, their Sunday best. And Friday night we went to synagogue, which wasn't very far from us. Talking about synagogues, Lodz had many synagogues, as you can imagine. And-- but three of them were very beautiful synagogues. One was not very far from us and it had a big dome. You could see it from miles away. And it had a-- there was a, a cantor and a choir. And it was beautiful inside. The, the, the, the decor-- decor-- decorations inside, the-- it was very elaborate. And in the morning, we didn't go to that synagogue. We went to a small shtieble. A shtieble is where usually there is a rabbi who is a descendent of a famous rabbi. And he has his own service in this place, where only Hasidim usually go to such a place. My father, though he was clean-shaven but on Shabbat, we-- he wore a silk garment. And we also had-- me and my brother, as little boys-- we also had such silk, little kapotes, they were called. And we only wore them when we went to this rabbi because it was the normal garb that one wore when you went to-- to see a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you-- would you have considered yourself Hasidim or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said before, my father was a businessman. But he basically came from Hasidim. So he carried-- he carried on the tradition as far as possible. He wanted to preserve the tradition. So that's why on Shabbat we used to wear these garments. But otherwise-- also, the school we went to was very-- it was under the auspices of the Agudat Yisrael, which is a very orthodox organization. And most of the children in that school, came from very Hasidic families. So I remember when my father used to come to visit-- to see us to speak to the teachers in school, I always felt embarrassed because he didn't have a beard. So I always used to feel ashamed that he didn't have a beard, for the other boys. They shouldn't tease me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tease you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them did, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they used to say that my father is a modern man, you know. He hadn't got a beard. So I used to say to them, it doesn't-- he doesn't-- it doesn't grow. He doesn't have a beard. It doesn't grow. [LAUGHS] Just as a joke.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school, when we had the, the secular lessons, we spoke Polish. But otherwise, we spoke Yiddish and at home, also. And most Jews in that-- in Lodz-- I don't know about other cities-- but in Lodz, most Jews spoke Yiddish. Jews that spoke Polish at home were already assimilated Jews, which didn't keep any-- much about-- they didn't keep kosher, or-- or they didn't keep the Shabbat. Such Jews, they spoke Polish. They wanted to assimilate and to be-- And I think, also, the reason maybe-- I mean, I wouldn't like to-- my-- from what I've learned over the years and what I've read, I think if you live in a country like England, for instance, most people, most Jews speak English at home, I'm told. I speak-- with my wife and with my children, I speak English, because I'm living in this country and I'm happy here. And I don't notice much antisemitism and the people are friendly, so I feel I-- I speak their language. Where in Poland, you tried to keep separate from the Poles. Because they didn't like us, and we didn't like them. I must say that. That's my opinion. Maybe I'm wrong. I was only a child. But I thought-- I think that was a reason why we only spoke Yiddish at home. We didn't speak Polish. Although we knew the language. I mean I went to school. I knew the language. I knew how to speak Polish. I spoke-- I played with the Polish children in the, in the, in the, in the, in the park and in the garden. And I spoke to them Polish. But at home, we only spoke Yiddish. I don't know why, but that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the relationships with the, the-- with the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were playing in the park with Polish kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, children are children. Children didn't know much unless-- if they had at home talk against the Jews, so they also used to come out with the-- sometimes with antisemitic words. But otherwise, children are children. We played together. [DRINKS] But, it's a fact that the Jews in Lodz got on better with the Germans before the war than they did Poles because, I think, of the language. You see, German is similar to Yiddish. So-- also, they were more connected in business as well. The Jews had more contact with the Germans in business than with the Poles. So I think that also made a big difference. But things completely changed in, in the late '30s when we, we-- there was talk about the war coming. So things completely changed. We became more friendly with the Poles because they felt we had more-- something in common against Hitler than the Germans. They were waiting for the-- for the-- for the-- for the Nazis to march in, see. So they kept away from the Jews. So the situation completely changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How Did you experience any antisemitism when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The first time I-- I experienced antisemitism, I was about eight years old. And we used to buy packets of coffee. And, you know, they had coupons on them. And if you collected enough coupons, you could take these coupons to a place and exchange them for-- for an album of labels of different kinds of animals. So after we had enough coupons, so we went along to this office from this coffee firm to exchange these labels-- these coupons to get the album with the labels.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- the office was already outside the Jewish area. There were no more Jews living there. And when we came near this office, some Polish boys around the same age as us started throwing stones at us. So we ran very fast away till we came back into the Jewish quarter. That's when I realized the first time that I'm a Jew and I'm being treated different, even by children. How did they see you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they could tell the way we were dressed. We used to have caps, different caps. The Polish schools had different caps to us. We, we had-- they could tell we are Jewish. Also, the attitude in Poland, it's hard to understand today. We were told on Sunday when we were on holiday-- we didn't notice it so much in town, but when we were on holiday-- we lived in the village, which was surrounded by Poles-- and we were told not to go out on Sunday at the time when the people come out from the churches. Because the-- the-- the church used to whip up the-- the Poles against the Jews. And then they came out, they started beating up Jews, whoever was around. So we were always told not to go out at that time, when the people come out from the churches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see this happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see people being whipped up by people that came from church?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, it was normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me-- could tell some, some more about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they used to-- they used to come out from the church and they-- whoever was in the-- whoever was about was beaten up. It happened very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: How could they see you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They could tell. They could tell. We dressed different, we, we, we spoke different. They could tell who was Jewish, who isn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you dress?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did Jews dress in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well on holiday, we-- we used run around in a pair of shorts and, and a shirt in the summer. But when we went to school, we used to wear such a little-- how should I call it-- long, long-- like overalls. They didn't have that, you see. So they could tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wear a yarmulke?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Either a yarmulke or a cap. The cap was different. In the street, we didn't wear yarmulkes. If you wore yarmulkes in the street, you asked for trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even in the Jewish neighborhoods?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even in the Jewish neighborhoods, we didn't wear yarmulkes. We had-- we had school caps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to boys who did wear yarmulkes in the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they got beaten up. It, it was quite normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what-- what did Jews do when they were beaten up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that is one of the problems. They-- they-- they turned the other cheek most of the time. I was always told when we were playing with Polish children, for instance, and if-- if ever an argument develops, and you have a bit of a fight, you know, like, with children, don't hit back. We were always told, don't shout back and don't hit back. Because if we hit back, a Polish child, the father used to come and bring the police. And you didn't stand a chance. I'll give you an example what happened. I remember as a child, there was not far from us a Jewish market, market where most of the stallholders were Jews. And a Polish youth, he was about 18 or 20 years old-- he was running with a, a revolver in his hand. And he was shooting into the crowd. Most of the-- of the people around there-- it was on a Thursday afternoon-- most of the Jews were shopping for the Shabbat. And he was-- he injured a few people, and he killed one or two. Finally, a Jewish butcher got hold of him and handed him over to the police. There was a big trial. It dragged on for about a year, maybe longer. And he was acquitted. This is-- was the Polish justice. A Jew did not stand much a chance in law. It's not like here. People today can't imagine these things, but Jews didn't have many rights in Poland. Especially after the-- 1935, the leader of the Polish government, Pilsudski his name was-- and he died. So then it changed. Things completely changed after his death.
